prevalence.population: Infants in the first year of life
prevalence.source: Pediatric allergy epidemiology syntheses; Indian pediatric allergy practice patterns
prevalence.trend: stable; increased recognition and dietary management in urban pediatrics

burden.dalyPerCapita: low mortality; caregiver and nutrition burden can be high
burden.mortalityRate: Very low; severe reactions possible but uncommon

riskFactors: cow’s milk exposure via formula or dairy
vulnerablePopulations: families without access to allergy specialists
households with cost barriers to hypoallergenic formulas
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical history; elimination and re-challenge; specific IgE/skin tests when IgE-mediated suspected; rule out infections and non-allergic intolerance

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; pediatric evaluation based on symptoms
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Hundreds of thousands of infants annually
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good with avoidance and appropriate formula; limited by cost and access

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Oral food challenge capacity mostly limited to tertiary centers
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Avoid cow’s milk protein; use extensively hydrolyzed or amino-acid formula when needed; nutrition counseling; supervised re-introduction to assess tolerance
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: High cost of specialized formulas, underdiagnosis in rural settings, confusion with lactose intolerance
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Practical dietary management and strong pediatric primary care pathways in many settings
